facts.text: Nashville was founded in 1779 as Fort Nashborough, named for Revolutionary War general Francis Nash, and became the state capital in 1843.
In 1925 the Grand Ole Opry opened, launching Nashville’s reputation as the Country Music Capital of the World.
The Fisk Jubilee Singers emerged after the Civil War, turning Fisk University into a beacon of hope and cultural pride.
Nashville’s Country Music Hall of Fame and Museum, Bridgestone Arena, and Nissan Stadium were all renovated or built during Governor Bredesen’s urban renewal push.
The city hosted nine Olympic soccer matches at Geodis Park during the 2028 Summer Olympics, marking its first major international sporting event.
Nashville’s Union Station once served as a major intercity rail hub for the Louisville and Nashville Railroad, reflecting the city’s early transportation history.
